questions.q: How did Maxwell predict the speed of electromagnetic waves?
options: By measuring the speed of light directly in his laboratory
From \(c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}\), using the permeability and permittivity of free space from electromagnetism experiments, which gave \(3\times10^{8}\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\)
By assuming light travels at the speed of sound
From the frequency of visible light multiplied by Planck's constant
questions.explanation: Maxwell's wave equation gives \(c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}\). These two constants come from electricity and magnetism experiments, yet the result equalled the measured speed of light — leading him to conclude light is an electromagnetic wave. Hertz confirmed it with radio waves in 1887.

questions.q: Which of the three wave-model pillars specifically proves that light is a transverse wave?
options: Diffraction
Interference
Polarisation
Reflection
questions.explanation: Diffraction and interference prove light is a wave but not its type. Only polarisation proves it is transverse, because only transverse waves can be polarised — a longitudinal wave (like sound) has no sideways plane to filter.

questions.q: In Young's double-slit experiment, why do dark fringes provide conclusive evidence for the wave model?
options: Because particles travelling in straight lines naturally leave gaps
Because two streams of particles can only ever add to more light, never cancel, but two waves with the right path difference cancel by destructive interference
Because the dark fringes prove light has mass
Because dark fringes appear only at the threshold frequency
questions.explanation: Particles can only add up to more light; they cannot cancel to darkness. Waves with a path difference of a whole number plus a half wavelength interfere destructively, producing dark fringes — something only a wave can do.

questions.q: Unpolarised light passes through a polariser, then an analyser at 60° to it. What fraction of the original intensity emerges? (\(\cos^2 60^\circ = 0.25\))
options: 25% of the original
12.5% of the original
50% of the original
questions.explanation: The first polariser cuts unpolarised light to exactly 50% (Malus's law does NOT apply to the first filter). Then the analyser applies Malus's law: \(0.5 \times \cos^2 60^\circ = 0.5 \times 0.25 = 0.125\), i.e. 12.5%.

questions.q: What was the ultraviolet catastrophe, and how did Planck resolve it?
options: Classical theory predicted infinite intensity at short wavelengths; Planck resolved it by quantising energy into packets of \(hf\)
Hot objects emitted no ultraviolet; Planck added a new force
Light was too bright to measure; Planck used filters
The Sun emitted dangerous UV; Planck blocked it with the aether
questions.explanation: Classical wave theory predicted black-body intensity rising without limit toward short wavelengths (infinite energy — absurd). Planck assumed energy is exchanged only in discrete quanta of \(hf\), which suppressed high-frequency emission and matched the peak-and-fall experimental curve.

questions.q: The wave model of the photoelectric effect explains how many of the four observations (no delay, threshold frequency, number from intensity, energy from frequency)?
options: All four
Only one — that intensity sets the number of photoelectrons
Three of four
None
questions.explanation: The wave model correctly predicts only that brighter light ejects more electrons. It fails on the other three: it predicts a delay (none observed), no threshold (one exists), and energy from intensity (energy actually comes from frequency). Einstein's photon model explains all four.

questions.q: What did the Michelson–Morley experiment find, and which model did it force?
options: A strong aether wind, supporting the wave model
A null result (no aether), forcing the constancy of the speed of light and special relativity
That light travels at 1.5c, supporting the particle model
Interference fringes proving light is transverse
questions.explanation: The experiment got a null result — no fringe shift, no aether wind. This killed the aether and supported Einstein's postulate that the speed of light is constant for all observers, the foundation of special relativity.

questions.q: Why do far more cosmic-ray muons reach the ground than their short half-life classically allows?
options: Muons are created at ground level
From Earth's frame time dilation lengthens their decay time; from the muon's frame the atmosphere is length-contracted — both frames predict more survive
Muons travel faster than light
The atmosphere has no effect on muons
questions.explanation: Both relativistic frames agree: Earth sees the muon's clock run slow (time dilation), the muon sees a contracted atmosphere (shorter journey). Each independently predicts many more muons survive, confirming relativity.

questions.q: A spacecraft of proper length 100 m moves at 0.8c relative to Earth (\(\gamma = 1.67\)). What length does an Earth observer measure, and which is the proper length?
options: 167 m measured; 100 m is the proper length
60 m measured; 100 m (the rest-frame length) is the proper length
60 m measured; 60 m is the proper length
100 m measured; no contraction occurs
questions.explanation: Contracted length \(= L_0/\gamma = 100/1.67 = 60\,\mathrm{m}\). The proper length is the 100 m measured in the spacecraft's own rest frame; the Earth observer measures the shorter 60 m.

questions.q: What is the unifying punchline of Module 7 — the single thing that both opened the module and forced its most radical consequence?
options: Planck's constant, which appears in every equation
The constant speed of light: Maxwell predicted it from electromagnetism to open the module, and its constancy (Einstein's first postulate) forced special relativity
The work function of metals
The double-slit interference pattern
questions.explanation: The same number — the speed of light — opened the module when Maxwell calculated it from electromagnetism, and became Einstein's first postulate, forcing time dilation, length contraction and mass-energy equivalence. Light is a wave that is also a particle, and its constant speed bends space and time.
